original_abstract: Based on the 2011 biennial review by the IUPAC Commission on Isotopic Abundances and Atomic Weights, the standard atomic weights of five elements have been revised. The atomic weight of germanium has been refined to 72.630(8), indium to 114.818(1), and mercury to 200.592(3). Most notably, the standard atomic weights for bromine and magnesium have been transitioned from single values to the intervals [79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307], respectively. These interval assignments are a deliberate shift to acknowledge that the isotopic composition of these elements varies significantly across common terrestrial materials, meaning their atomic weights are not universal constants but are dependent on the geological or biological source of the sample.
humanized_abstract: In the 2011 biennial review by the IUPAC Commission on Isotopic Abundances and Atomic Weights the standard atomic weights of five elements were revised. The new values for the atomic weights of germanium, indium and mercury are 72.630(8), 114.818(1) and 200.592(3) respectively. The standard atomic weights for bromine and magnesium have been changed to the ranges [79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307] respectively. The reason for the change in these elements is that the composition can differ greatly in different terrestrial samples, and hence the atomic weight of the element in a sample can be an artifact of the source of the sample (geological or biological) rather than a true and constant constant for the element.

The standard atomic weights for bromine and magnesium have been changed to the ranges [79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307] respectively. The reason for the change in these elements is that the composition can differ greatly in different terrestrial samples, and hence the atomic weight of the element in a sample can be an artifact of the source of the sample (geological or biological) rather than a true and constant constant for the element.

Most notably, the standard atomic weights for bromine and magnesium have been transitioned from single values to the intervals [79.901, 79.907] and [24.304, 24.307], respectively. These interval assignments are a deliberate shift to acknowledge that the isotopic composition of these elements varies significantly across common terrestrial materials, meaning their atomic weights are not universal constants but are dependent on the geological or biological source of the sample.
